1.pericope_label: Jesus, Mediator of a Better Covenant
1.exposition: With a single word — kephalaion, “the main point” — the writer gathers seven chapters of argument to their crown: we have a high priest who has sat down at the right hand of the throne of the Majesty in heaven. Everything now turns on where he ministers. The priests of Aaron serve in a tent pitched by human hands; Christ serves in the true tabernacle the Lord set up, not man. Because a high priest must have something to offer (v.3), and because the earthly office was already full and closed to a man of Judah (v.4), his priesthood belongs not to earth but to heaven, of which Moses' tabernacle was only a copy and shadow. And so the reasoning arrives at its thesis: a more excellent ministry, because a better covenant, enacted on better promises.
1.original_language: The section opens on kephalaion (v.1) — a word meaning both “summary” and “chief point,” and, as Robertson notes, the pith of the argument in either sense. Christ is a leitourgos (“public minister,” one who performs sacred service on the people's behalf, v.2) in the skēnē alēthinē, the “true” — that is, real as against merely typical — tabernacle. The Levitical priests serve a hypodeigma kai skia (“copy and shadow,” v.5) built to the typos (“pattern, model”) shown Moses (Exodus 25:40). In v.6 Christ is mesitēs (“mediator, go-between”) of a kreittōn (“better”) covenant — the comparative that tolls through the whole homily.
1.historical_context: The claim that if Christ were on earth he would not be a priest is a frank admission: within the Aaronic system Jesus, sprung from the tribe of Judah (7:14), held no priestly credentials at all. His priesthood operates in the realm the earthly one only copies. The “true tabernacle which the Lord set up” draws on a strand of Second-Temple Jewish thought that pictured a heavenly sanctuary answering to the earthly, rooted in the Exodus mandate that Moses build “according to the pattern shown you on the mountain” (Exodus 25:40). That the writer speaks of the Levitical priests in the present tense — those who “offer the gifts” — suggests the temple still stood as he wrote.
1.christ: Chrysostom notes how the writer “mixes the lowly with the lofty”: the same Jesus who suffered in the flesh is now the enthroned minister of heaven. The very posture is gospel — the priests of Aaron stood daily to repeat their work, but Christ sat down, because his one offering is finished. He is at once the sacrificing priest with “something to offer” (v.3), the minister of the real sanctuary, and the mediator of the better covenant — priest, offering, and covenant-maker in one person.

verses.note: The writer gathers his whole argument to a head: we have such a high priest, seated at the right hand of the throne of the Majesty in heaven. grammatical-historical Robertson and Barnes weigh the word kephalaion — not a mere recap but the chief point, the pith of everything said. eastern Chrysostom seizes on the paradox: one who is seated is no ordinary minister, for the writer had already said the angels stand and serve while the Son is bidden to sit (1:13) — the enthroned priest whose atoning work is done. wesleyan-arminian Wesley adds simply that he sat down “having finished his oblation.”

He is a minister of the sanctuary and of the true tabernacle, which the Lord set up, not man. grammatical-historical Clarke hears in leitourgos a “public minister” who transacts the people's business with God, as Christ transacts the whole race's business in the heavenly original the earthly tent only copied; Robertson notes skēnē alēthinē, the “genuine” tabernacle of which the wilderness tent was but the symbol. reformed Calvin guards the contrast — the old tabernacle was no human invention but a true “effigy” of the heavenly; yet as shadow is to substance, it was not the reality Christ now serves.

Every high priest is appointed to offer gifts and sacrifices; so this priest too must have something to offer. reformed Calvin draws the sober inference: a priesthood is an empty name without a sacrifice, and every acceptable approach to God rests on one — which exposes the folly of praying while forgetting the death of Christ. grammatical-historical Barnes agrees the offering here is unique: unlike the priests who bring the blood of beasts, Christ's “something to offer” is himself, his own blood (9:12).

If he were on earth, he would not be a priest at all, since there are already priests who offer the gifts according to the law. grammatical-historical Barnes and Clarke note the plain force — the earthly office was full, occupied by Levites, and Jesus came from Judah, not Levi (7:14); on earth he would not even qualify. reformed Calvin presses the alternative sharply: either Christ is no priest while the Levitical order stands, or that order is now abolished — and since to deny Christ his priesthood is impiety, the Levitical priesthood must be at an end.

The Levitical priests serve a copy and shadow of the heavenly things, as Moses was warned when about to erect the tent: “See that you make everything according to the pattern shown you on the mountain” (Exodus 25:40). grammatical-historical Robertson folds hypodeigma and skia into a single “shadowy outline,” and marks typos, the model Moses was to copy. reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical Gill agree the command itself is the argument: if the tent had to match a heavenly pattern, the earthly service was never the reality but a genuine representation pointing beyond itself.

The hinge of the section: Christ has obtained a ministry as much more excellent as the covenant he mediates is better, since it is enacted on better promises. reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical Barnes tie the superiority together — the ministry is greater in exact proportion as the covenant is greater, and the word mesitēs names Christ the go-between who reconciles the two parties. puritan Matthew Henry identifies the “better promises” as the very blessings the oracle is about to name — the law on the heart, the knowledge of God, and free forgiveness (vv.10–12).

witnesses.point: The writer “mixes the lowly with the lofty”: the Son who suffered is now the seated minister of heaven, and one who sits is no mere servant but is himself served.
kephalaion is the “pith” of the whole argument — Christ is a better priest, in a better sanctuary, with a better sacrifice, a better covenant, and better promises.
Christ's priesthood is heavenly and spiritual; his ascension proves it, and by his coming the earthly Levitical priesthood is made void.
We have a high priest such as no age of the church ever had; the “better promises” are forgiveness, the law written on the heart, and the knowledge of God.

external.name: Harold W. Attridge
external.point: The language of “copy and shadow” locates the earthly cult in a realm derivative of the heavenly, so the full weight of the argument falls on the reality of Christ's priesthood exercised in the true, transcendent sanctuary.

1.application: Our high priest is seated — his work is done. The Christian does not approach God hoping some sacrifice may yet be completed, but rests on one already finished and forever pleaded in heaven.

7.exposition: Having named the “better covenant,” the writer now proves it from Scripture — and lets the proof run to unusual length. The logic is compact: a faultless covenant would need no successor, yet God himself, finding fault, announced one. What follows is the whole of Jeremiah 31:31–34, the longest Old Testament quotation in the New Testament, cited as the charter of everything the letter is claiming. The old covenant was broken by the very people it was given to (v.9); the new answers each failure — the law written within instead of on stone, God's people bound to him in mutual belonging, a knowledge of God shared by all from least to greatest, and sins remembered no more (vv.10–12). The very word “new,” the writer concludes, has already pronounced the first covenant old and ready to vanish.
7.original_language: In v.7 the first covenant is weighed as amemptos (“faultless, without defect”) and found wanting — not sinful, but unable to perfect. The oracle promises a kainē diathēkē (v.8), a covenant “new” in kind, not merely renewed; and diathēkē itself carries the double sense of “covenant” and “will/testament” the letter will exploit in the next chapter. The closing verb is palaioō (“to make old, render obsolete,” v.13): by speaking of a “new” covenant God has already antiquated the first, and what is aged is near aphanismos — disappearance.
7.historical_context: Jeremiah's new-covenant oracle (Jeremiah 31:31–34) was spoken in the shadow of the Babylonian exile, after Israel's persistent breaking of the Sinai covenant — a promise that God would one day write his law on hearts rather than on tablets of stone. Its inclusion here is remarkable on its own terms: this is the longest single Old Testament quotation in the New Testament, deployed as the covenant-charter of the entire argument. The promise that “they shall no longer teach one another” does not abolish the teaching office but describes a shift from mediated knowledge — through priests and scribes — to an inward, direct knowledge of God. Its summit, “I will remember their sins no more,” stands over against the old order's annual reminder of sins on the Day of Atonement (10:3).
7.christ: To quote the whole Jeremiah oracle is to make a quiet christological claim: this is precisely the covenant Christ mediates (v.6), and its promises are “better” because they rest on his better sacrifice. The old covenant's repeated offerings produced a repeated remembrance of sin; the new covenant's “I will remember their sins no more” is possible only because, as the letter will argue, “by a single offering he has perfected for all time those who are being sanctified” (10:14). Jeremiah wrote the charter toward the future; Christ is its fulfillment.

verses.note: Had that first covenant been faultless, no place would have been sought for a second. grammatical-historical Robertson and Barnes read amemptos carefully: the old covenant was not sinful but defective — weak, unable to perfect (7:18–19) — and the mere search for a replacement is itself the verdict on it. puritan Matthew Henry puts it pointedly: that God sought room for a second proves the first insufficient.

Finding fault with them, God says: the days are coming when I will make a new covenant with the house of Israel and the house of Judah. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the phrase “finding fault with them” leaves the ambiguity of the Greek intact — the fault lies with the people who broke the covenant, and so with the covenant that could not hold them. Here begins the citation of Jeremiah 31:31–34: a kainē diathēkē, a covenant fresh in kind, promised through the prophet in exile.

The new covenant is not like the one God made with the fathers when he took them by the hand to lead them out of Egypt — “for they did not continue in my covenant, and I paid them no heed.” reformed Calvin hears tenderness in the figure — God leading Israel as a father leads a child by the hand — and yet the covenant was broken. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson mark the tragic sequence: the people did not abide, and God in consequence turned away; the old covenant failed through human unfaithfulness, and that failure is why a better, inward one was needed.

The first great promise: I will put my laws into their minds and write them on their hearts, and I will be their God and they shall be my people. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson set the contrast against Sinai — the old law was cut in stone and stood outside; the new is inscribed within, on mind and heart. reformed Calvin names the agent: this inward writing is the Spirit's regenerating work, so that obedience springs not from external compulsion but from a renewed heart — the great superiority of the new arrangement.

They shall not teach one another, “Know the Lord,” for all shall know me, from the least to the greatest. grammatical-historical Barnes reads this not as the abolition of teaching but as the promise that saving knowledge of God will no longer be the preserve of a priestly or learned few; every member of the covenant, high and low, will know him. The knowledge meant is inward and personal — a real acquaintance with God that the old economy's external instruction did not universally impart.

The ground of all the rest: I will be merciful toward their iniquities, and I will remember their sins no more. grammatical-historical Barnes and reformed Calvin stress the completeness — God's forgiveness is so full that he treats the pardoned sins as if forgotten, never raising them again, unlike the old covenant's yearly remembrance of sins (10:3). The little word “for” makes forgiveness the root: because God pardons fully, he writes his law within and gives the knowledge of himself.

In calling this covenant “new,” God has made the first old; and what is becoming obsolete and growing aged is ready to vanish away. grammatical-historical Robertson and Barnes catch the logic hidden in a single word: to name one covenant new is to pronounce the other palaios, antiquated. reformed Calvin adds the image of a worn garment nearing its end; and since the letter was written before AD 70, several of the witnesses hear a possible hint of the temple's approaching fall and the final passing of the old order.

witnesses.point: The very promise of a “second” covenant proves the first defective; it was not sinful but weak — unable to perfect — and its people broke it, so God turned away.
The law written on the heart is the Spirit's regenerating work: obedience that flows from a renewed heart rather than outward compulsion, the great superiority of the new covenant.
That God sought place for a second covenant is itself the verdict that the first was insufficient; the new rests on better promises — pardon, the inward law, the knowledge of God.

external.name: William L. Lane
external.point: The full citation of Jeremiah's oracle serves as the scriptural centerpiece of the letter's covenant argument, its climax reached in the divine pledge to remember sins no more — a decisive, unrepeatable forgiveness the old cult could not give.

7.application: The best of the new covenant's promises is its last and deepest: “I will remember their sins no more.” Everything else — the law within, the knowledge of God — grows from that root of full and final forgiveness. Live today as one whose account is closed.

_meta.title: Mediator of a Better Covenant
reflection: Our high priest “sat down” because his work was finished (v.1). Where are you still living as though your acceptance with God depended on a sacrifice you must keep completing?
The new covenant writes God's law “on their hearts” rather than on stone (v.10; Jeremiah 31:33). What changes when obedience is understood to flow from a renewed heart rather than an external rule?
God promises, “I will remember their sins no more” (v.12). Is there a forgiven sin you keep bringing back to mind that God has already closed the account on?
